title: WordPress 7.0 Ships with AI Foundations in Core, a Modernized Admin, and New Design Tools
gist: WordPress 7.0 ships AI foundations directly in core — an AI Client, Abilities API, Connectors hub, and an MCP Adapter letting agents like Claude Code and Codex read and write sites — alongside a modernized admin and design tools, splitting the community over the platform's AI direction.

insights.claim: WordPress 7.0 introduces AI directly in core — an AI Client, an Abilities API, and a Connectors hub where admins authenticate external AI providers in a few clicks, plus an optional official AI plugin for generating images, titles, excerpts, and alt text.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: The web's largest CMS baking in AI infrastructure creates a distribution channel and a new plugin surface for AI providers.

insights.claim: An official MCP Adapter lets agents such as Cursor, Claude Code, and Codex read and write to WordPress sites directly.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: Direct agent write-access to a huge installed base is a major MCP adoption vector — and a security surface to watch.
insights.quote: agents such as Cursor, Claude Code, and Codex read and write to sites directly

insights.claim: One developer argued the Connectors screen is a red herring and the real advance is a PHP AI SDK usable by any plugin, paired with the Abilities API.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: The reusable AI SDK, not the visible UI, may be the durable platform primitive — where third-party value accrues.
insights.quote: shipped something much better: a PHP AI SDK that can be used by any plugin

insights.claim: WordPress 7.0, named after Louis Armstrong, shipped May 20, 2026 after slipping from an April 9 target, opening Gutenberg Phase 3 with 420+ enhancements from more than 875 contributors, over 200 of them first-timers.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: A major, collaboration-focused release cycle signals continued platform momentum despite AI controversy.
insights.locator: WordPress 7.0, named after jazz legend Louis Armstrong

insights.claim: The upgrade raises the minimum PHP requirement to 7.4, and the long-anticipated real-time collaboration feature was pulled from 7.0 over performance and server-load concerns.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: Real-time collaboration slipping again signals the technical hard problem; watch 7.1 (expected August 2026).
insights.locator: The upgrade is not without friction.

insights.claim: New design tooling includes a universal Command Palette via Cmd/Ctrl+K, content-only pattern editing, a revisions panel for templates and patterns, the font library across all themes, and a new @wordpress/grid package.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: Incremental workflow polish praised as 'compounding' value for agencies managing many client sites.
insights.locator: Design and editing see attention too.

insights.claim: Community reaction split largely over the AI direction, with one long-time user posting 'I don't want integrated AI' and another saying they would migrate sites to the ClassicPress fork.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: AI-in-core is driving a visible fork/migration risk — ClassicPress positions as the AI-free alternative.
insights.quote: I've been a WordPress user for years. I don't want integrated AI

insights.claim: Developers flagged safety questions around AI write access and practical breakage, with one user reporting the update changed their site's styling.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: AI write access plus upgrade breakage are the concrete adoption risks enterprises will weigh before upgrading.

insights.claim: For teams weighing alternatives, ClassicPress offers an AI-free, block-optional option while Drupal and Ghost remain the usual comparisons for publishers wanting a different governance model.
insights.speaker: Daniel Curtis
insights.so_what: Maps the CMS competitive set for AI-averse buyers — governance model becomes a differentiator.
